attributes.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with a two-week history of sensory disturbance in the upper limbs. She also noticed weakness in her hands. She had no relevant medical history. Neurologic evaluation reveals mild weakness and wasting of the small muscle of the hands and reduced upper limb reflexes. Temperature and pain sensation is impaired throughout the upper limbs and upper trunk. Lower limb motor and sensory examination is normal. Plantar responses are down going. MRI of the cervical spine is done (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The patient's presentation with suspended sensory loss and lower motor neuron weakness in the upper limb would suggest central cord lesion. The MRI shows dilated central canal and herniation of the cerebellar tonsils known as " Arnold-Chiari malformation". These findings are suggestive of syringomyelia which classically presents as a capelike area of sensory loss to pain and temperature over the upper trunk and arms at the level of the lesion (due to affection of the decussating spinothalamic tract fibers). A characteristic pattern of distal greater than proximal weakness in the upper limbs predominantly the hands (due to damage to the anterior horn cells at that level) ensues.

Anterior spinal artery occlusion causes anterior cord syndrome (damage to the ventral cord) which manifests as pyramidal motor weakness and, often, bilateral loss of pain and temperature sensation below the level of the lesion. Proprioception and vibration are preserved as the dorsal columns run posteriorly in the cord.

Brown-Séquard syndrome refers to ipsilateral upper motor neuron motor weakness, proprioceptive deficits and impaired appreciation of vibration, with contralateral pain and temperature loss. It is caused by a lesion that involves the lateral half of the cord, affecting the corticospinal, spinothalamic, and, sometimes, proprioceptive tracts on that side.

Complete cord transection manifests as bilateral upper motor neuron (UMN) pattern weakness and bilateral loss of all types of sensation below level of the lesion. It may be caused by trauma, demyelination, or hemorrhage.

A posterior column syndrome occurs when pathology of one or both dorsal columns of the spinal cord is present. Patients may present with sensory ataxia and have proprioceptive deficits on examination as well as impaired appreciation of vibration. The prototypic cause is vitamin B12 deficiency, leading to subacute combined degeneration of the cord in which the lateral columns are also involved concurrently.
learningPoints: Syringomyelia is a rare neurologic disorder, characterized by the slow development of fluid-filled areas extending along the spinal cord, and causing central cord syndrome.

One of the causes of syringomyelia is Arnold- Chiari malformation which is a congenital abnormality in which cerebellar tonsils herniate through the foramen magnum in the spinal canal, with altered CSF flow.

At first the central cord lesion disrupts bilateral-crossing spinothalamic fibers passing in the anterior commissure, causing loss of pain and temperature in one or more adjacent dermatomes bilaterally at the level of the lesion. sensation above and below the lesion remains intact, leading to a ‘‘suspended’’ sensory level.
As a central lesion enlarges, it encroaches on anterior horn cells, causing segmental lower motor neuron weakness at the level of the lesion.
Further expansion affects the lateral corticospinal tracts and anterolateral tracts, causing upper motor neuron weakness and temperature and sensation loss below the lesion.
references: Cho TA. Spinal cord functional anatomy. CONTINUUM Lifelong Learning in Neurology. 2015 ;21:13–35.
